strategy_research:
{
  "strategies": [
    {
      "name": "Keller Vigilant Asset Allocation (VAA-G4)",
      "description": "Breadth momentum strategy using a universe of risk assets (SPY, QQQ, IWM, EFA) plus defensive assets (TLT, AGG, GLD). Computes a weighted 13612W momentum score overweighting recent months. Allocates 100% to the top-ranked risk asset when all risk assets have positive momentum; shifts proportionally to defensive assets (AGG/TLT/GLD) as breadth deteriorates. Typically 50%+ in cash/bonds during drawdowns, enabling vigorous crash protection.",
      "evidence": [
        "Keller & Keuning (2017) 'Breadth Momentum and Vigilant Asset Allocation: Winning More by Losing Less', SSRN #3002624",
        "AllocateSmartly independent replication and live tracking (allocatesmartly.com)",
        "Extended to DAA (Defensive Asset Allocation) in Keller & Keuning (2018), SSRN #3212862 — canary universe improvement"
      ],
      "historical_sharpe": "0.94 (VAA-G4, 1970–2016 backtest); CAGR 18.9%, MaxDD 13.0%, MAR 1.46",
      "market_conditions": "Excels during trending markets and bear markets due to fast breadth signal. Underperforms in whipsaw/choppy sideways markets due to high turnover. Works best when asset class divergence is high. Struggles in V-shaped recoveries where reentry lags.",
      "instruments": ["SPY", "QQQ", "IWM", "EFA", "TLT", "AGG", "GLD"],
      "timeframe": "monthly",
      "complexity": "medium"
    },
    {
      "name": "Antonacci Global Equity Momentum (GEM / Dual Momentum)",
      "description": "Two-step momentum filter applied monthly: (1) Absolute momentum — if SPY 12-month return exceeds T-bills, stay in equities; otherwise go to AGG. (2) Relative momentum — if in equities, choose SPY vs. EFA (international) by 12-month relative return. Only 3 ETFs, ~2–3 trades per year. Combines trend-following (crash protection) with relative strength (best equity exposure).",
      "evidence": [
        "Antonacci (2012) 'Risk Premia Harvesting Through Dual Momentum', Portfolio Management Consultants",
        "Antonacci (2014) 'Dual Momentum Investing', McGraw-Hill — extended backtest 1950–2018",
        "InvestResolve (2019) 'Global Equity Momentum: A Craftsman's Perspective' — fragility analysis",
        "Composite Dual Momentum live tracking on AllocateSmartly — Sharpe 1.02, UPI 3.10"
      ],
      "historical_sharpe": "0.85–1.02 depending on implementation (Composite version); GEM base ~0.75–0.90. MaxDD ~25–30% vs ~55% buy-and-hold.",
      "market_conditions": "Works well in sustained trends up or down. Fails in rapid reversals (March 2020 recovery) and choppy regimes. Lookback sensitivity is a known fragility — 9-month vs 10-month lookback can produce dramatically different returns in the same year.",
      "instruments": ["SPY", "EFA", "AGG"],
      "timeframe": "monthly",
      "complexity": "low"
    },
    {
      "name": "Cross-Asset Momentum Rotation with Trend Filter + Vol Targeting",
      "description": "Rank all instruments by 1–12 month momentum score (e.g., 3/6/12 month equally weighted). Hold top 2–4 ranked assets. Apply absolute trend filter (price > 200-day SMA) — if asset fails filter, replace with AGG or cash. Scale each position to target equal volatility contribution (e.g., 10% annualized vol each). Rebalance monthly. Combines relative momentum, trend following, and volatility normalization.",
      "evidence": [
        "Moskowitz, Ooi & Pedersen (2011) 'Time Series Momentum', Journal of Financial Economics — validated across 58 assets",
        "Hurst, Ooi & Pedersen (2012) 'A Century of Evidence on Trend-Following Investing', AQR — 137 years, positive in 8/10 worst 60/40 drawdowns",
        "Quantpedia Strategy #6 'Asset Class Momentum Rotational System' — ~5% annual excess return vs benchmark",
        "Man Group (2017) 'The Impact of Volatility Targeting' — vol targeting improves Sharpe for equity-heavy multi-asset portfolios",
        "CME Group (2015) 'Momentum Strategies Across Asset Classes' — EMV-weighted cross-asset Sharpe 0.78 (1972–2014)"
      ],
      "historical_sharpe": "0.95–1.25 (combined trend + momentum + vol targeting); cross-asset momentum alone ~0.78",
      "market_conditions": "Excels in trending markets across asset classes. Benefits from crisis periods (trend to bonds/gold). Underperforms in flat, mean-reverting environments. Vol targeting reduces crash severity. Historically positive in 8 of 10 worst equity drawdowns.",
      "instruments": ["SPY", "QQQ", "IWM", "EFA", "GLD", "TLT", "AGG"],
      "timeframe": "monthly",
      "complexity": "medium"
    },
    {
      "name": "Volatility-Targeted Risk Parity (Equal Risk Contribution)",
      "description": "Allocate to each asset such that each contributes equally to total portfolio volatility (Equal Risk Contribution). Scale overall portfolio to a target volatility (e.g., 10–12% annualized) using a vol-scaling multiplier based on realized 21-day rolling volatility. Rebalance monthly when weights drift >5% or vol regime changes. Does not require return forecasts — pure diversification play. For $1000 account, cap leverage at 1.0x (no leverage version retains diversification benefit without margin).",
      "evidence": [
        "Asness, Frazzini & Pedersen (2011) 'Leverage Aversion and Risk Parity', Financial Analysts Journal",
        "AQR White Paper 'Understanding Risk Parity' — higher Sharpe than 60/40 over multiple regimes",
        "S&P Dow Jones Indices 'Indexing Risk Parity Strategies' — risk parity indices outperform 60/40 on Sharpe",
        "Moreira & Muir (2017) 'Volatility-Managed Portfolios', Journal of Finance — vol targeting raises Sharpe for equity market factor",
        "Man Group (2017) 'The Impact of Volatility Targeting' — consistent improvement across 60+ assets"
      ],
      "historical_sharpe": "0.80–1.05 (unleveraged); leveraged versions historically 1.0–1.3. Unleveraged returns lower than 60/40 but Sharpe higher. Vulnerable to correlation breakdowns (COVID March 2020, 2022 inflation shock).",
      "market_conditions": "Works best in normal correlation regimes (stocks and bonds negatively correlated). Fails when correlations converge to 1 (March 2020, 2022 taper/inflation). Volatility targeting layer partially mitigates drawdowns by reducing exposure ahead of spikes.",
      "instruments": ["SPY", "TLT", "AGG", "GLD", "EFA"],
      "timeframe": "monthly",
      "complexity": "medium"
    },
    {
      "name": "Regime-Based Rotation (VIX Terciles + Yield Curve)",
      "description": "Classify market regime monthly using two signals: (1) VIX level relative to trailing percentiles (tercile method: low <33rd pct = risk-on, mid = neutral, high >67th pct = risk-off); (2) 10Y–2Y yield curve slope direction. Risk-on: overweight SPY/QQQ/IWM/EFA; Risk-off: rotate to TLT/AGG/GLD. Neutral: balanced allocation. Optionally add HY credit spread signal (OAS > 400bps = defensive). Rebalance when regime changes.",
      "evidence": [
        "Nature Scientific Reports (2025) — VIX tercile segmentation achieves Sharpe 1.17 vs 0.94 for fixed thresholds, 24% improvement",
        "Gilchrist & Zakrajsek (2012) 'Credit Spreads and Business Cycle Fluctuations', American Economic Review — HY spreads predicted 16/18 recessions with 4-month lead",
        "MSCI (2018) 'Adaptive Multi-Factor Allocation' — VIX curve shape drives factor rotation",
        "ArXiv (2024) 'Dynamic Asset Allocation with Asset-Specific Regime Forecasts' — regime-aware portfolio 137% cumulative vs 73.8% S&P 500 benchmark"
      ],
      "historical_sharpe": "0.85–1.17 (regime-aware multi-asset); ML-enhanced versions in high-vol regimes show 187% higher Sharpe vs static risk parity",
      "market_conditions": "Works well when macro regime transitions are gradual and identifiable. Fails when regimes shift rapidly (flash crashes, sudden policy pivots). Yield curve signal has long lead times (~4 months) which helps but can also cause early positioning.",
      "instruments": ["SPY", "QQQ", "IWM", "EFA", "TLT", "AGG", "GLD"],
      "timeframe": "monthly",
      "complexity": "medium"
    },
    {
      "name": "Hierarchical Risk Parity (HRP) with Momentum Tilt",
      "description": "Construct portfolio weights using Lopez de Prado's HRP algorithm: (1) compute correlation matrix of all instruments, (2) apply hierarchical clustering to group similar assets, (3) allocate via recursive bisection so clusters compete for risk budget rather than individual assets. Augment with 3-month momentum tilt — within each cluster, overweight the higher-momentum asset. No matrix inversion required; robust to ill-conditioned covariances. Rebalance monthly.",
      "evidence": [
        "Lopez de Prado (2016) 'Building Diversified Portfolios that Outperform Out-of-Sample', Journal of Portfolio Management 42(4) — HRP achieves lower out-of-sample variance than CLA despite CLA minimizing variance",
        "ResearchGate (2020) 'Machine Learning Portfolio Optimization: HRP and Modern Portfolio Theory' — HRP higher Sharpe than MPT out-of-sample",
        "Springer Annals of Operations Research (2026) 'Fast Hierarchical Risk Parity Methods' — scalability and robustness confirmed"
      ],
      "historical_sharpe": "0.85–1.10 out-of-sample in simulations; consistently outperforms equal-weight and minimum-variance on drawdown and Sharpe in reported studies",
      "market_conditions": "Most robust in varied correlation regimes due to no matrix inversion requirement. Benefits from genuine asset class diversification (stocks, bonds, gold, international). Momentum tilt improves returns in trending regimes. Underperforms when all assets crash simultaneously.",
      "instruments": ["SPY", "QQQ", "IWM", "EFA", "GLD", "TLT", "AGG"],
      "timeframe": "monthly",
      "complexity": "high"
    },
    {
      "name": "Stacked Signal Ensemble (Momentum + Trend + Carry Proxy)",
      "description": "Combine three independent weak signals into a single allocation score per asset: (1) Cross-sectional momentum rank (3/6/12-month average return rank across all instruments); (2) Time-series trend signal (price vs. 10-month SMA, binary +1/0); (3) Carry proxy (TLT/AGG carry estimated from yield, GLD carry negative, equities estimated by earnings yield). Aggregate signals with equal weight, then apply volatility targeting to normalize positions. Run ensemble over multiple lookback windows (3, 6, 9, 12 months) and average allocations. Rebalance monthly.",
      "evidence": [
        "Newfound Research (2019) 'Ensemble Multi-Asset Momentum' — ensemble Sharpe in top quartile, MaxDD in bottom quartile across 500+ specifications",
        "CME Group paper (2015) — combining carry, value, momentum raises Sharpe to ~1.37–1.40 in time-series; 3Y rolling Sharpe always positive",
        "Goldman Sachs AM (2018) 'How to Combine Investment Signals' — mixed signal combination outperforms integrated on Sharpe",
        "ScienceDirect (2025) 'Characteristic-Managed Momentum' — stacked approach raises Sharpe from 0.49 to 1.47 vs standard momentum"
      ],
      "historical_sharpe": "1.20–1.47 for well-implemented stacked approaches; ensemble averaging specifically delivers top-quartile Sharpe with bottom-quartile MaxDD",
      "market_conditions": "Most robust strategy family — diversification across signal types reduces single-signal failure risk. Carry signal helps in low-trend sideways markets. Trend signal protects in crashes. Momentum signal captures sustained moves. Still underperforms in sharp, rapid reversals.",
      "instruments": ["SPY", "QQQ", "IWM", "EFA", "GLD", "TLT", "AGG"],
      "timeframe": "monthly",
      "complexity": "high"
    },
    {
      "name": "Credit-Spread-Signaled Bond-Equity Rotation",
      "description": "Use HY credit spreads (ICE BofA OAS, available via FRED) as a leading macro signal with ~4-month lead on equity drawdowns. Rules: when HY OAS crosses above 400bps or 12-month moving average, rotate from SPY/QQQ to TLT/AGG/GLD. When HY OAS compresses below threshold, rotate back to equities. Augment with VIX > 25 as a confirming signal. This cross-asset lead-lag exploits the information advantage of the credit market which has historically signaled 16 of 18 recessions before equity peaks.",
      "evidence": [
        "Gilchrist & Zakrajsek (2012) 'Credit Spreads and Business Cycle Fluctuations', American Economic Review — HY OAS predicted 16/18 recessions, avg 4-month lead",
        "Neuberger Berman 'The Importance of Monitoring Credit Spreads' — negative correlation between OAS and equity performance",
        "Acharya & Johnson (2007) — CDS market leads equity in informed trading conditions",
        "Federal Reserve FEDS (2012) 'Credit Spreads as Predictors of Real-Time Economic Activity' — Bayesian model confirms predictive power"
      ],
      "historical_sharpe": "0.80–1.05 estimated; primary value is crash avoidance (4-month lead enables proactive defensive rotation rather than reactive)",
      "market_conditions": "Most valuable ahead of credit-driven bear markets (2000–2002, 2007–2009, 2015–2016, 2020). Less useful in valuation-driven corrections or sudden external shocks. Credit spreads can stay wide for extended periods without immediate equity declines, causing opportunity cost.",
      "instruments": ["SPY", "QQQ", "TLT", "AGG", "GLD"],
      "timeframe": "monthly",
      "complexity": "medium"
    }
  ],
  "recommendation": "Start with the Stacked Signal Ensemble (Strategy 7) or VAA-G4 (Strategy 1) depending on implementation appetite. VAA-G4 is the best first implementation for a $1000 account: it is fully defined by a published formula, requires only monthly rebalancing, has the strongest documented backtest (Sharpe 0.94, MaxDD 13% over 46 years), and maps directly onto the available ETF universe (SPY/QQQ/IWM/EFA + TLT/AGG/GLD). The 13612W momentum score is a single formula requiring only monthly closing prices. For a more sophisticated approach with higher expected Sharpe (1.20–1.47), implement the Stacked Signal Ensemble — but this requires maintaining three concurrent signal calculations. The Regime-Based Rotation (Strategy 5) is a strong complement: use VIX tercile + yield curve regime as a meta-filter to scale down all risk allocations when VIX is in the top tercile, layered on top of VAA. This combination — VAA core + regime overlay — targets Sharpe above 1.0 with MaxDD below 15% and is implementable with monthly rebalancing at any account size."
}
```

Sources:
- [Keller & Keuning (2017) VAA Paper — SSRN #3002624](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=3002624)
- [AllocateSmartly — Vigilant Asset Allocation](https://allocatesmartly.com/vigilant-asset-allocation-dr-wouter-keller-jw-keuning/)
- [Keller & Keuning (2018) DAA Paper — SSRN #3212862](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/Delivery.cfm/SSRN_ID3307823_code1935527.pdf?abstractid=3212862)
- [AllocateSmartly — Composite Dual Momentum](https://allocatesmartly.com/antonaccis-composite-dual-momentum/)
- [Newfound Research — Fragility Case Study: Dual Momentum GEM](https://blog.thinknewfound.com/2019/01/fragility-case-study-dual-momentum-gem/)
- [InvestResolve — Global Equity Momentum Executive Summary](https://investresolve.com/global-equity-momentum-executive-summary/)
- [AQR — Understanding Risk Parity](https://www.aqr.com/-/media/AQR/Documents/Insights/White-Papers/Understanding-Risk-Parity.pdf)
- [Man Group — The Impact of Volatility Targeting](https://www.man.com/insights/the-impact-of-volatility-targeting)
- [AQR / Hurst et al. — A Century of Evidence on Trend-Following Investing](https://fairmodel.econ.yale.edu/ec439/hurst.pdf)
- [CME Group — Momentum Strategies Across Asset Classes](https://www.cmegroup.com/education/files/jpm-momentum-strategies-2015-04-15-1681565.pdf)
- [Lopez de Prado (2016) HRP Paper — SSRN #2708678](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=2708678)
- [Nature Scientific Reports (2025) — ML Regime-Based Asset Allocation](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-26337-x)
- [Newfound Research — Ensemble Multi-Asset Momentum](https://blog.thinknewfound.com/2019/07/ensemble-multi-asset-momentum/)
- [CME Group — Dissecting Investment Strategies Cross-Section & Time-Series](https://www.cmegroup.com/education/files/dissecting-investment-strategies-in-the-cross-section-and-time-series.pdf)
- [Gilchrist & Zakrajsek — Credit Spreads and Business Cycle Fluctuations (Chicago)](https://mfm.uchicago.edu/wp-content/uploads/2020/07/Gilchrist_Zakrajsek_Credit-Spreads-and-Business-Cycle-Fluctuations-UPDATED.pdf)
- [Quantpedia — Asset Class Momentum Rotational System](https://quantpedia.com/strategies/asset-class-momentum-rotational-system)
